confidence.disclosure: Unlike Ibn Sina or Ibn Arabi, Ibn Rushd left no autobiography. Most of what we know comes from later biographers — al-Ansari, Ibn al-Abbar, al-Marrakushi — supplemented by the dates of his official appointments under the Almohad caliphs, which are reliable. His birth on 14 April 1126 in Cordoba and his death on 11 December 1198 in Marrakesh are known to the day. The years of his judgeships in Seville (from 1169) and Cordoba (from 1171), his appointment as physician to Caliph Abu Yaqub Yusuf in 1182, the exile to Lucena in 1195, and the recall in 1197 are all attested in the chronicles. The intermediate movements between Cordoba and Marrakesh in his earlier career are not always datable; this file errs on the side of fewer entries with higher confidence.

sources: Al-Ansari, biographical entry on Ibn Rushd
Abd al-Wahid al-Marrakushi, al-Mu'jib fi Talkhis Akhbar al-Maghrib
Majid Fakhry, Averroes: His Life, Works and Influence (2001)

journey.event: Born on 14 April 1126 in Cordoba into one of the most distinguished legal families of al-Andalus. His grandfather Abu al-Walid Muhammad was chief judge of Cordoba and imam of its great mosque under the Almoravids; his father Abu al-Qasim Ahmad held the same office.
Studies hadith, jurisprudence, theology and medicine in Cordoba. Reportedly receives his legal license (ijaza) at age sixteen. Studies medicine under Abu Jafar Harun of Trujillo.
The Almoravid dynasty under which his family had served falls to the rising Almohads. The Ibn Rushd family quickly aligns with the new regime; their legal expertise makes them valuable to any ruler.

Travels to Marrakesh, capital of the Almohad caliphate, and comes under the patronage of the founding caliph Abd al-Mu'min — likely involved in establishing educational institutions for the new dynasty. Conducts astronomical observations there.

Introduced to Caliph Abu Yaqub Yusuf by the philosopher and physician Ibn Tufayl (author of Hayy ibn Yaqzan). The caliph, himself a student of philosophy, asks Ibn Rushd whether the heavens are created or eternal. Ibn Rushd, terrified at first, eventually realises the caliph wants real philosophy, and is commissioned to write commentaries that will make Aristotle accessible.

Appointed qadi (judge) of Seville. Begins the great project of his life — the short, middle and long commentaries on Aristotle that will make him 'the Commentator' to medieval Europe. Writes that the heat and the bustle of Seville left him little time for reading.
Transferred to Cordoba as judge — the office his grandfather had held. Remains here for ten years, producing a steady stream of legal, medical and philosophical works.

Composes the three closely linked religious-philosophical treatises that defend the legitimacy of philosophy: Fasl al-Maqal (The Decisive Treatise), its appendix on theology, and the great Tahafut al-Tahafut (The Incoherence of the Incoherence) — his point-by-point refutation of al-Ghazali's earlier attack on the philosophers.
Called to Marrakesh to succeed the dying Ibn Tufayl as personal physician to Caliph Abu Yaqub Yusuf.
Abu Yaqub Yusuf dies and is succeeded by his son Abu Yusuf Yaqub al-Mansur. Ibn Rushd retains the new caliph's favour and is sent back to Cordoba as Chief Judge.

Serves as chief qadi of Cordoba — one of the highest offices in the Almohad realm — while continuing his vast philosophical output. Through these years he revises his Aristotelian commentaries, perhaps trying to reconcile them with the Almohad creed.

Sudden disgrace. As the caliph al-Mansur prepares for jihad against the Christian kingdoms of the north, he needs the support of the Maliki traditionalists who hate philosophy. Ibn Rushd is denounced, his books on philosophy are publicly burned, and he is exiled to Lucena, a largely Jewish village some 60 km southeast of Cordoba.

Days after Ibn Rushd's exile, Caliph al-Mansur wins his great victory over the Christian forces of Alfonso VIII at Alarcos on 19 July 1195 — the last triumph of Muslim arms in the Iberian peninsula. With the threat past, the caliph no longer needs to appease the jurists.
Recalled from exile and summoned back to the caliph's presence in Marrakesh. Restored to favour but a broken man.

Dies in Marrakesh on 11 December 1198 (9 Safar 595 AH), perhaps still under house arrest. His body is later transferred for burial in the family tomb in Cordoba — a journey at which the young Ibn Arabi is present, and which he describes as a poignant lesson in the imbalance between a man's books on one side of the mule and his coffin on the other.
